\chapter{Boolean Analysis — Restriction Compose}
%%% generated by the blueprint pipeline from TCSlib.BooleanAnalysis.LMN.RestrictionCompose
\section{Overview}

This module shows that Bernoulli random restrictions compose: applying a
Bernoulli($p$) restriction and then a Bernoulli($q$) restriction on the remaining
free variables yields the same distribution as a single Bernoulli($pq$) restriction.
It introduces the composition of two restrictions and the per-variable weight, and
builds up through factorization and fiber-weight identities to the main composition
equality and its probabilistic inequality corollary.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Declarations}

\begin{definition}[Composition of two restrictions]
\lean{LMN.composeRestr}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.Restriction}
Given two restrictions $\rho_1, \rho_2$ on $n$ variables, their composition is the
restriction that on each coordinate $i$ uses the value fixed by $\rho_1$ if there is
one, and otherwise the value given by $\rho_2$. This models applying $\rho_1$ first
and then $\rho_2$ on the variables left free by $\rho_1$.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Per-variable Bernoulli weight]
\lean{LMN.varWeight}
\leanok
For a parameter $p \in \bbr$, the per-variable weight assigns $p$ to a free
coordinate (value \texttt{none}) and $(1-p)/2$ to a fixed coordinate (value
\texttt{some\_}), matching the marginal of the Bernoulli restriction model.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Pointwise characterization of composition]
\lean{LMN.composeRestr_eq_iff}
\leanok
\uses{LMN.composeRestr, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction}
The composition $\mathrm{composeRestr}\,\rho_1\,\rho_2$ equals $\sigma$ if and only if
for every coordinate $i$ the value $(\rho_1\,i)$, falling back to $(\rho_2\,i)$,
equals $\sigma\,i$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Right identity for composition]
\lean{LMN.composeRestr_id_right}
\leanok
\uses{LMN.composeRestr, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction}
Composing a restriction $\rho$ with the all-free restriction (which fixes no
variable) returns $\rho$ unchanged.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Bernoulli weight factors as a product]
\lean{LMN.bernoulliRestrWeight_eq_prod}
\leanok
\uses{LMN.varWeight, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrWeight}
The Bernoulli restriction weight of $\rho$ at parameter $p$ equals the product over
all coordinates $i$ of the per-variable weights:
\[
  \mathrm{bernoulliRestrWeight}\,p\,\rho = \prod_{i : \mathrm{Fin}\,n} \mathrm{varWeight}\,p\,(\rho\,i).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Per-variable composition identity]
\lean{LMN.varWeight_compose_sum}
\leanok
\uses{LMN.varWeight}
For parameters $p, q \in \bbr$ and any outcome $c$, summing the products
$\mathrm{varWeight}\,p\,a \cdot \mathrm{varWeight}\,q\,b$ over all pairs $(a,b)$ whose
composition equals $c$ gives $\mathrm{varWeight}\,(pq)\,c$:
\[
  \sum_{a} \sum_{b} \mathrm{varWeight}\,p\,a \cdot \mathrm{varWeight}\,q\,b \cdot
    [\,a \,\triangleright\, b = c\,] = \mathrm{varWeight}\,(pq)\,c.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Fiber weight identity]
\lean{LMN.compose_fiber_weight_eq}
\leanok
\uses{LMN.bernoulliRestrWeight_eq_prod, LMN.composeRestr, LMN.varWeight, LMN.varWeight_compose_sum, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrWeight}
Summing the product $\mathrm{bernoulliRestrWeight}\,p\,\rho_1 \cdot
\mathrm{bernoulliRestrWeight}\,q\,\rho_2$ over all pairs $(\rho_1, \rho_2)$ that
compose to $\sigma$ equals the Bernoulli weight of $\sigma$ at parameter $pq$:
\[
  \sum_{\rho_1}\sum_{\rho_2} \mathrm{bernoulliRestrWeight}\,p\,\rho_1 \cdot
    \mathrm{bernoulliRestrWeight}\,q\,\rho_2 \cdot
    [\,\mathrm{composeRestr}\,\rho_1\,\rho_2 = \sigma\,]
  = \mathrm{bernoulliRestrWeight}\,(pq)\,\sigma.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[Composition of Bernoulli random restrictions, equality]
\lean{LMN.restriction_compose_eq}
\leanok
\uses{LMN.composeRestr, LMN.compose_fiber_weight_eq, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrWeight}
For $0 < p \le 1$, $0 < q \le 1$ and any event on restrictions, the Bernoulli($pq$)
probability of the event equals the weighted average, over Bernoulli($p$)
restrictions $\rho_1$, of the Bernoulli($q$) probability that the event holds after
composing with $\rho_1$:
\[
  \mathrm{bernoulliRestrProb}\,(pq)\,\mathrm{event}
  = \sum_{\rho_1} \mathrm{bernoulliRestrWeight}\,p\,\rho_1 \cdot
    \mathrm{bernoulliRestrProb}\,q\,\bigl(\rho_2 \mapsto \mathrm{event}(\mathrm{composeRestr}\,\rho_1\,\rho_2)\bigr).
\]
This says a Bernoulli($p$) restriction followed by a Bernoulli($q$) restriction on
the remaining free variables has the same distribution as a single Bernoulli($pq$)
restriction.
\end{theorem}

\begin{theorem}[Composition inequality]
\lean{LMN.restriction_compose_le}
\leanok
\uses{LMN.composeRestr, LMN.restriction_compose_eq, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb_le_one', SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrWeight_nonneg'}
For $0 < p \le 1$ and $0 < q \le 1$, the Bernoulli($pq$) probability of an event is
at most the Bernoulli($p$) probability that some $\rho_2$ makes the event hold after
composition:
\[
  \mathrm{bernoulliRestrProb}\,(pq)\,\mathrm{event}
  \le \mathrm{bernoulliRestrProb}\,p\,\bigl(\rho_1 \mapsto \exists \rho_2,\;
      \mathrm{event}(\mathrm{composeRestr}\,\rho_1\,\rho_2)\bigr).
\]
\end{theorem}
